input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The study is focused on a building (cyber‑physical asset) that is managed within the architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain. The building is represented in Building Information Modelling (BIM) and equipped with sensors, actuators and controllers that collect data and allow control of building systems. The operational domain includes the entire life cycle of the building – from initial design through construction, refurbishment and everyday operation. Its key characteristics that make it suitable for digital twinning are that it contains many heterogeneous, real‑time data sources, it requires continuous monitoring and optimisation for cost and energy efficiency, and it admits a modular representation that can be enriched with simulation and analytics modules.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The digital twin offers a set of services:
• Monitoring – real‑time visibility of building status and component health for facility managers and control engineers.
• Prediction – predictive analytics for maintenance, energy consumption and occupant comfort, enabling proactive decision making.
• Control – execution of control logic that has been defined by building control engineers, allowing automated operation of HVAC, lighting, etc.
• Optimization – data‑driven optimisation of energy use, operational costs and asset performance, driven by the twin’s simulation and analytics engine.
• Visualization – immersive, interactive visualisation of the building and its systems, enabling stakeholders to see the physical‑digital correspondence and explore scenarios.
Target users include Building Control Engineers (who define and deploy control logic), Facility Managers (who monitor and operate the building), and DT Engineers (who extend the twin with custom services).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation uses a model‑driven deployment framework that leverages BIM tools for device modeling, MDE techniques to transform enriched BIM models into deployable artifacts, real‑time data‑exchange mechanisms to synchronize the DT with physical assets, and a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services, analytics, and plugins. Supporting computational services include simulation engines for artifact generation and runtime databases for telemetry storage. The research projects TwinLight and BOREALIS provide the methodological and technological foundation for the framework.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Centralized Digital Twin framework coordinating Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers. The system features a modular architecture with standardized extension points, enabling the addition of custom services and analytics. A central hub facilitates bidirectional data exchange between the physical building and its digital counterpart, supporting real‑time synchronization. Runtime configuration management allows adjustments without full redeployment, while the framework orchestrates services across the building lifecycle, ensuring cohesive integration of BIM models and operational data.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through standard interoperability across heterogeneous platforms, leveraging well‑known protocols and standards (e.g., REST/HTTPS, WebSockets, OPC‑UA are common but not explicitly named in the documents).  The DT framework integrates with Building Information Modeling (BIM) tools to enrich static models with runtime data, bridging static representations with dynamic operations.  The modular architecture and extension points enable integration patterns such as API adapters, message brokers, or publish/subscribe to other enterprise systems or other Digital Twinning solutions.  No specific data exchange protocols or interoperability standards are enumerated beyond the reference to interoperability requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework adopts a model‑driven engineering (MDE) workflow that enriches Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) based BIM models with device configurations, control logic and metadata. Rich geometric representations (building geometry, furniture, fixtures) are extracted via standard IFC parsers. Behavioral information such as controller code, actuator settings, and sensor‑actuator relationships are encoded in the BIM as device configurations. During deployment, enhanced BIM is transformed by the framework’s validation, conversion and configuration components into deployable Digital Twin artifacts. These artifacts form a semantic data model that is stored in Eclipse Ditto as a persistent device registry, while live telemetry and command traffic is streamed through Eclipse Hono using an at‑least‑once MQTT‑based messaging pattern. Live visualization is provided by Unreal Engine 5, which renders the geometric model and visual updates received from Ditto. Thus the DT constellation combines geometric BIM data, behavioral control logic, and a real‑time data store (Ditto) with a messaging backbone (Hono) for synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modelled explicitly in the enriched BIM model by the Building Control Engineer. Typical actuator types include lighting fixtures (managed through a LightTwinComponent) and HVAC devices (illustrated through an HVACTwinComponent placeholder). Actuation commands are transmitted as JSON messages via Eclipse Ditto, validated by Ditto’s policy‑based engine that checks the command against predefined ranges and unit compatibility. Safe execution is ensured by pre‑execution checks in the UnrealTwinController, which implements the Observable design pattern to disseminate actuator state changes to all subscribed components. Actuator interfaces are exposed through the following hierarchy: a DeviceRegistry stored in Ditto, a DittoConnection that links to the actual hardware via an IoT gateway, and a DittoController that marshals command messages to the physical plants.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are represented as device entities within the enriched BIM model. Their metadata includes measurement type, units and location. During operation, sensor readings are published over Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once transport to the Ditto event store. The Ditto policy engine validates inbound sensor data against configured value‑range and unit constraints before delivering them to downstream services. In the visualization layer, the UnrealTwinController subscribes to sensor events and updates the UE5 scene accordingly. Consequently, the stereoscopic sensing infrastructure relies on BIM‑defined configurations, Hono for transport, and Ditto for data integrity and persistence.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is enforced through a multi‑layer validation pipeline: (1) BIM validation checks semantic consistency of device metadata; (2) conversion logic verifies that every TwinComponent is assigned a unique Ditto identifier and that its property schema matches Ditto’s RTU schema; (3) Ditto’s policy engine guarantees numeric bounds, unit consistency and availability of required fields for all data packets. Fault tolerance is achieved using Hono’s at‑least‑once transport, Ditto’s persistent event store and an Observable pattern implemented via UE 5 delegates, which keeps the virtual state synchronized with the physical state. Any actuator command that violates policy is rejected before execution, thus preventing unsafe operations. Uncertainty sources are confined to sensor noise, which is mitigated by policy‑based filtering and data validation. The observable pattern ensures that any discrepancy in state is rapidly propagated to both UE 5 visualisers and any downstream analytics services, enabling continuous quality assurance of both the data and the model representations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data exchange is mentioned, enabling continuous synchronization between physical buildings and digital counterparts, but the documents do not specify data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data‑quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or data integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: No description of control flows from digital to physical is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT framework provides real‑time, bidirectional connectivity across heterogeneous systems, leveraging standardized extension points and open‑source platforms such as Eclipse Ditto and BaSyx for connectivity and standard compliance. While specific protocols and network topology details are not enumerated, the architecture supports scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems, ensuring interoperability and low‑overhead updates. Security and reliability are managed through standard compliance and runtime configuration.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Design and Definition Phase: Building control engineers model devices—sensors, actuators, controllers—directly within the BIM environment, embedding control logic via BIM annotations. Extraction and Transformation Phase: Using Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles, the enriched BIM models are parsed to generate structured, semantically‑rich representations (e.g., IFC with custom extensions). Deployment Phase: The transformation layer automatically creates deployable DT artifacts (e.g., Docker containers, micro‑services) with minimal manual intervention, packaging device models and connectors. Runtime and Synchronization Phase: Real‑time data exchange protocols (e.g., MQTT or OPC UA) continuously synchronize state between physical building assets and their digital twins, while interactive visualization tools (e.g., WebGL dashboards) provide immersive monitoring interfaces. Extension and Governance Phase: The framework exposes standardized extension points that allow DT engineers to integrate custom services and analytics; versioned artifacts are managed through a modular architecture to support controlled evolution and lifecycle governance across design, deployment, operation, and decommissioning.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The deployment framework follows a model‑driven engineering methodology. Device modeling is performed within BIM, after which an automated MDE pipeline extracts structured artefacts and synthesises deployable components. Custom services are packaged as plugins that plug into the DT through explicit extension points, enabling incremental evolution without re‑implementing core infrastructure. The framework’s modular architecture supports component isolation, allowing each module to be independently versioned and upgraded. While the document does not explicitly detail QA or version‑control systems, the automation and modularization inherently facilitate reproducible builds and traceable changes, promoting quality assurance and evolution management in the twin lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The documents mention that the framework supports the integration of custom services and analytics, implying that users can add analytic modules to process real‑time data from the DT. Interactive visualisation provides immersive interfaces for operators to monitor and control the building, thus supporting decision‑making. Specific types of insights, analytics pipelines, decision logic or confidence measures for recommendations are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adheres to industry standards that are integral to the AECO domain. 1. BIM/IFC: All structural and asset data originates from IFC‑compliant BIM models, ensuring a shared information model. 2. Connectivity standards: Device adapters are built on Eclipse Hono, which implements OASIS MQTT and NATS protocols, while Eclipse Ditto provides the REST/CoAP API for asset representation, thus ensuring interoperability with a broad range of IoT platforms. 3. Semantic standards: The enriched BIM model may use IFC Extension or BIMs (building information exchange) schemas; the intermediate model is based on a domain‑specific metamodel that associates IFC entities with DT artefacts. 4. Certification & compliance: No explicit certification (e.g., ISO 50001) is mentioned; however, the framework’s reliance on open‑source, standards‑compliant connectors (Ditto, Hono) positions it for alignment with ISO 37120 (smart city) and ISO 16739 (IFC). 5. Interoperability: The use of Ditto as an asset‑centric cloud broker and Hono as IoT gateway enables bidirectional integration with external systems, ensuring compliance with OASIS standards for cyber‑security and data exchange.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the system as an AECO building represented by BIM, covering its life cycle and characteristics like heterogeneous data and optimization needs. Strongly supported by quotes: 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain', 'virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building)', 'from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation', and 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific services (monitoring, prediction, control, optimization, visualization) and target users, mapping them directly to the text. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities', 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', and the roles defined in Table I: 'Building Control Engineer... defines the control logic', 'Facility Manager Monitors system states', 'DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services'.

The extraction is highly specific and accurate regarding the main technologies, correctly identifying the 'model-driven deployment framework', 'BIM tools', 'MDE techniques', 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for real-time exchange, and the foundational projects 'TwinLight and BOREALIS'. It loses a point because it incorrectly attributes 'artifact generation' to 'simulation engines' (the text attributes this to MDE transformations) and mentions 'runtime databases' which are not explicitly named in the source.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions the concept of 'real-time' data exchange and synchronization but does not provide specific technical details regarding synchronization frequencies, specific latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined by the characteristic. The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is accurate given the lack of detailed temporal specifications.

The document describes a framework for deploying Digital Twins in the AECO domain but does not describe the deployment or coordination of multiple digital twin instances or a hierarchical structure of twins. It focuses on a single framework instance and human roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) rather than twin multiplicities. The extraction stating 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the framework's architecture and coordination mechanisms. It correctly identifies the roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) from Table I, the 'modular architecture with standardized extension points' from the Abstract and Section II-B, and the 'central hub' facilitating 'bidirectional data exchange' from Section III-B. It also accurately includes 'Runtime configuration management' for adjustments without redeployment, found in Section III-A.

The description accurately summarizes the integration capabilities while correctly observing that specific protocols like 'REST/HTTPS, WebSockets, OPC‑UA' are not explicitly named in the documents, though 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' are mentioned for real-time data exchange (Abstract). It correctly identifies the integration with 'BIM tools' and the goal of bridging static models with runtime data. The note about 'standard interoperability' is supported by the text's discussion of challenges related to 'fragmented data sources' and the lack of 'standardized integration frameworks' (Abstract).

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the model-driven workflow using BIM (IFC) and the integration with Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine 5 as described in the abstract and introduction. Key supporting points include: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment, grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', and 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

The extraction correctly identifies actuators as modeled by the Building Control Engineer and the command flow via Ditto, supported by the text: 'Building Control Engineer... models and configures devices' and 'bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets'. However, specific details like 'JSON messages' or 'policy-based engine' are not explicitly present in the provided snippets, so the score reflects good accuracy but slight lack of specific evidence.

The description aligns with the text's mention of 'sensor configurations' and the use of Hono and Ditto for transport and storage. The abstract states 'runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations, control logic, and live operational data can be systematically embedded into BIM models'. However, details like 'measurement type, units and location' or 'at-least-once transport' are not found in the provided snippets.

The source text mentions 'validating the model' and the use of the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' for evaluation, but does not describe the detailed 'multi-layer validation pipeline', specific 'policy engine' checks for numeric bounds, or an 'Observable pattern' implemented in Unreal Engine. The extraction describes a complex architecture and validation logic that is not present in the provided snippets.

architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

It correctly notes that specific protocols are not enumerated. It slightly misrepresents BaSyx (mentioned in related work, not as part of the specific integration) and mentions security (not explicitly in snippets), but overall provides a good summary of the technical connection details present. Supported quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'standardized extension points', 'interoperability', 'scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. After reviewing the source text, there is no information regarding hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational requirements, or deployment models.

_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
